block_00: Lyft may be in need of a “lift”
block_01: Lyft has reported a disappointing start to their public debut; the company officially went public in March & has since lost around 29% of its initial value.
block_02: According to Quartz, a global business news organization based in New York; there is a belief that Lyft deliberately disguised the company’s drop, by ignoring the $1.1 billion of losses under GAAP, or “Generally Accepted Accounting Principles”, which was mainly due to a $894 million ‘stock-based compensation” & other related payroll-tax expenses.
block_03: The company’s CFO, Brian Roberts maintained “we have teams across the company dedicated to initiatives that will help us grow more profitably“ & Lyft is “aggressively investing” in new businesses like bikes & scooters.
block_04: Some industry professionals feel the company’s path to profitability remains tremendously unclear & that the CFO delivered a vague response to the reported losses.
block_05: Lyft is currently trading at just over $51 per stock, which is $21 down from the initial price of $72 when it launched.
block_06: The RSI indicates the stock is oversold, it could lead to a potential reversal in the market.
